era_context: Late Bronze Age Levant and Eastern Mediterranean. The Hittite Empire under Šuppiluliuma I is consolidating power in Anatolia. Egypt is in the early 18th Dynasty under Ahmose I or his immediate successors, having recently expelled the Hyksos. Mycenaean Greece is in the Middle Bronze Age (Middle Helladic III). Babylon has fallen to the Hittites; Kassite rule is establishing itself in southern Mesopotamia. This is a period of significant political reorganisation across the Eastern Mediterranean and Near East.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records for this period)
East Asia (Chinese records exist but are sparse for this specific year)
Mesoamerica (no written records; archaeological context only)
Northern Europe (no written records; archaeological inference only)

events.title: Hittite consolidation under Šuppiluliuma I's reign
events.description: Šuppiluliuma I is in the early-to-middle phase of his reign, which lasted approximately 1344–1322 BCE (conventional dating). He is consolidating Hittite power in Anatolia and beginning campaigns to expand Hittite influence into Syria and the Levant. This period marks the rise of the Hittite Empire as a major Near Eastern power. The exact events of 1625 BCE are not individually documented, but this year falls within the broader context of Hittite expansion.

sources.name: Hittite royal annals and treaties (later compilation, 13th century BCE)

events.certainty_note: Šuppiluliuma I's reign is conventionally dated to c. 1344–1322 BCE, but absolute chronology for this period remains debated. 1625 BCE (proleptic Gregorian) corresponds to approximately the 18th century BCE in conventional Near Eastern chronology. The assignment of specific events to this year is speculative.

events.title: Egyptian 18th Dynasty consolidation post-Hyksos expulsion
events.description: Egypt under the early 18th Dynasty (Ahmose I and successors) is consolidating control following the expulsion of the Hyksos from the Delta. This period marks the beginning of the New Kingdom and Egyptian imperial expansion. The exact chronology of this period is contested; conventional dating places Ahmose I's reign c. 1550–1525 BCE, but alternative chronologies exist. This year falls within the broader era of Egyptian state reorganisation and military consolidation.
key_figures: Ahmose I (Egyptian pharaoh, if still reigning)

sources.name: Egyptian king lists and temple inscriptions (later compilation and contemporary epigraphic)
Historiographic consensus on Egyptian New Kingdom chronology

events.certainty_note: Egyptian chronology for the 18th Dynasty is debated. Conventional dating places Ahmose I c. 1550–1525 BCE, but some scholars propose dates as late as 1540–1510 BCE. The assignment of 1625 BCE to this period is speculative and depends on which chronological system is adopted.

disconfirming_evidence: The conventional chronology for the 18th Dynasty and Hittite Empire is contested. Some scholars (e.g., proponents of the 'low chronology') place these events significantly later than traditional dating. The 'high chronology' places them earlier. No specific events are reliably attested for the year 1625 BCE itself; the entries above describe broader contextual processes. The reliability of assigning events to individual years in this period is extremely limited.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1625 BCE is extremely sparse. No contemporary written sources directly document events of this specific year. Our knowledge comes from later compilations (royal annals, king lists), archaeological stratigraphy, and historiographic reconstruction. The chronology of the Late Bronze Age is notoriously uncertain, with debates spanning 100+ years. Egyptian and Hittite sources are biased toward royal and military narratives. The experiences of common people, trade networks, and non-state societies are largely invisible. Sub-Saharan Africa, East Asia, and the Americas have no written records for this period.

graph_edges.note: Šuppiluliuma I's reign spans multiple years; events in 1620 BCE would fall within the same reign.
18th Dynasty consolidation is a multi-year process spanning this period.
